skill.name: test-orchestrator
skill.description: Coordinates software testing strategy across unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and security validation work.
skill.summary: Plan, coordinate, and evaluate software test coverage across multiple test types.
skill.author: Brownbull

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks, ordinary documentation text, and example code as risky patterns. Manual review found no executable skill code, no command execution instruction, no weak cryptographic implementation, and no prompt injection attempt.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The flagged locations are Markdown fences, inline skill names, directory tree text, or non-executed examples. No evidence found that the skill executes shell commands or directs command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are visible Markdown formatting or illustrative snippets, not Ruby or shell execution. The file contains no implementation that can run those examples automatically.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain the skill description and a test quality checklist item. No evidence found of cryptographic algorithms, hashing APIs, password handling, or encryption logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is front matter and line 131 is documentation about descriptive test names. Neither line contains cryptographic code or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The flagged line is a documentation heading for testing anti-patterns. No evidence found of host inspection, user enumeration, environment probing, or system information collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a list of testing practices to avoid. It does not instruct the agent to inspect the system or collect local machine details.

content.value_statement: Teams often miss test gaps when unit, integration, end-to-end, and performance work are planned separately. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code organize testing priorities, coverage targets, quality gates, and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Creates test plans across unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and security categories.
Defines coverage goals, acceptance criteria, and quality gate expectations.
Coordinates testing tasks across specialized test skills or testing roles.
Reviews test failures, coverage metrics, and flaky test indicators.
Documents test plans, result summaries, and quality metrics for project handoff.
limitations: It provides testing guidance and coordination, not a runnable test framework.
It does not include scripts, fixtures, or project-specific test commands.
It relies on the user or agent to inspect the target codebase before implementation.
It does not verify real coverage unless test results are provided.

use_cases.title: Plan Release Testing
use_cases.description: Create a structured test plan before a release, including scope, risk areas, coverage goals, and acceptance criteria.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Test Implementation
use_cases.description: Break testing work into unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and security tasks for a feature team.

use_cases.title: Review Quality Gates
use_cases.description: Evaluate failures, coverage gaps, flaky tests, and build-blocking conditions before merging a change.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Test Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a test plan for this feature. Include unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and security checks. Define acceptance criteria and coverage goals.

prompt_templates.title: Find Coverage Gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current tests and identify coverage gaps. Group findings by test type, risk level, and recommended next action.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Test Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Assigning testing tasks across a team
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this feature scope into testing tasks. Assign each task to the right testing category and include expected inputs, outputs, and completion criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Quality Gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced CI and release readiness review
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a quality gate for this release. Include required test suites, coverage thresholds, failure handling, flaky test policy, and release-blocking conditions.

output_examples.input: Plan testing for a password reset feature.
output_examples.output: A test plan covering token generation, email delivery, expired links, invalid tokens, rate limits, audit logging, and end-to-end recovery flow.

output_examples.input: Review these coverage results before merge.
output: Highlights uncovered authentication branches and missing API error tests.
Flags one flaky browser test for quarantine and root cause review.
Recommends blocking merge until critical path coverage reaches the target.

output_examples.input: Create a release quality gate for checkout changes.
output_examples.output: A release checklist with required unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and security validations tied to clear pass or fail criteria.

best_practices: Provide feature scope, risk areas, existing test results, and coverage targets before asking for a plan.
Keep quality gates measurable with clear pass or fail criteria.
Review flaky tests separately from product failures so release risk is visible.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for broad test coverage without naming the component or user flow.
Do not treat coverage percentage as proof that critical behavior is tested.
Do not ignore failing or flaky tests when defining release readiness.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It coordinates test strategy and can help plan commands, but it does not provide executable automation.

faq.question: Which test types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and security testing coordination.

faq.question: Can it enforce coverage requirements?
faq.answer: It can define and review coverage targets, but actual enforcement depends on your CI and test tooling.

faq.question: Is it useful without an existing test suite?
faq.answer: Yes. It can create an initial test plan and prioritize the first tests for high-risk behavior.

faq.question: Can it help with flaky tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It can classify flaky tests, suggest retry policy, and separate instability from product defects.

faq.question: Does it require a specific programming language?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is language-agnostic and should be adapted to the project test framework.
